The magnitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-component of vector is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></math>. The magnitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-component of the vector will be :
question: A vector in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi></math> plane makes an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>30</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis. The magnitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-component of vector is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></math>. The magnitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-component of the vector will be :
solution:
The angle made by the vector on x axis is 

θ=90°-30°=60°

Let the vector be a→.

It is given that

 ay=23⇒asin60°=23⇒a32=23⇒a=4

x component is ax=acos60°⇒ax=4×12=2

question: A student measures the time period of 100 oscillations of a simple pendulum four times. The data set is 90 s, 91 s, 95 s and 92 s. If the minimum division in the measuring clock is 1 s, then the reported mean time should be:
solution:
T T s T i - T
  t 1  90  - 2
  t 2  91  - 1
  t 3  95  3
  t 4  92 0 
  T i  92  Σ|Ti-T|N=1.5

Tr = 9 2 ± 1.5

Tr = 9 2 ± 2

Because least count of clock is 1s

question: The sum of two forces <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> such that <math><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math>. Find the angle between resultant of <math><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> , ________
solution:
So angle between 2P→+Q→ and Q→ is 90o


Alternate solution


P→+Q→=P→
P2+Q2+2PQcos⁡θ=P2
⇒Q+2PQcos⁡θ=O
⇒cos⁡θ=-Q2P

tan⁡α=2Psin⁡θ2Pcos⁡θ+Q=∞
∵2Pcos⁡θ+Q=0
α=90o

q:
The following observations were taken for determining surface tension T of water by capillary method:

diameter of capillary, D=1.25×10-2 m

rise of water, h=1.45×10-2 m

Using g=9.80 m s-2 and the simplified relation T=rhg2×103 N m-1 the possible error in surface tension is closest to:
question:
The following observations were taken for determining surface tension T of water by capillary method:

diameter of capillary, D=1.25×10-2 m

rise of water, h=1.45×10-2 m

Using g=9.80 m s-2 and the simplified relation T=rhg2×103 N m-1 the possible error in surface tension is closest to:
solution:
Percentage error in surface tension 

∆TT=∆rr+∆hh

∆rr=∆DD=0.011.25

∆hh=0.011.45

∴% error in T=∆TT×100=0.011.25+0.011.45×100=1.489≈1.5

q: Three particles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are moving along the vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mo>  </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>C</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>, respectively. They strike on a point and start to move in different directions. Now particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is moving normal to the plane which contains vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> Similarly particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is moving normal to the plane which contains vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>C</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> The angle between the direction of motion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt></mfrac></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><menclose notation="bottom"><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo></menclose></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>.</mo></math>
question: Three particles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are moving along the vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mo>  </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>C</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>, respectively. They strike on a point and start to move in different directions. Now particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is moving normal to the plane which contains vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> Similarly particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is moving normal to the plane which contains vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>C</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> The angle between the direction of motion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt></mfrac></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><menclose notation="bottom"><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo></menclose></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Direction of P, v^1=±A→×B→|A→×B→|=±i^-j^+k^3

Direction of Q, v^2=±A→×C→|A→×C→|=±2k^2=±k^

Angle between v^1 and v^2,

v^1·v^2v^1v^2=±13(1)(1)=±13

⇒x=3

subject: Physics
q: The energy of a system is given as $\mathrm{E}(\mathrm{t})=\alpha^3 \mathrm{e}^{-\beta t}$, where t is the time and $\beta=0.3 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The errors in the measurement of $\alpha$ and $t$ are $1.2 \%$ and $1.6 \%$, respectively. At $t=5 \mathrm{~s}$, maximum percentage error in the energy is :
question: The energy of a system is given as $\mathrm{E}(\mathrm{t})=\alpha^3 \mathrm{e}^{-\beta t}$, where t is the time and $\beta=0.3 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The errors in the measurement of $\alpha$ and $t$ are $1.2 \%$ and $1.6 \%$, respectively. At $t=5 \mathrm{~s}$, maximum percentage error in the energy is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& d E=3 \alpha^2 d \alpha e^{-\beta t}+\alpha^3 e^{-\beta t}(-\beta) d t \\
& \frac{d E}{E}=\frac{3 d \alpha}{\alpha}+(-\beta) d t \\
& \frac{\Delta E}{E}=\frac{3 \Delta \alpha}{\alpha}+\beta \Delta t
\end{aligned}$
(\%) $\Delta t=1.6 \% \times 5=8 \%$
$\%\left(\frac{3 \Delta \alpha}{\alpha}\right)=3 \times 1.2 \%=3.6 \%$
Putting values we get
$\Rightarrow 3.6+8 \times 0.3=6 \%$

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following relations is true for two unit vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> making an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> to each other?
question: Which of the following relations is true for two unit vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> making an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> to each other?
solution:
We know,

 1- cos θ=2sin2θ21+ cos θ=2cos2θ2

Now,a→+b→·a→+b→=a→2+2a→·b→+b→2⇒a→+b→·a→+b→=12+2×1×1×cosθ+12 ⇒a→+b→·a→+b→=21+cosθ⇒a→+b→2=4cos2θ2⇒a→+b→=2cosθ2  ...(1)

Similarly,

a→-b→·a→-b→=a→2-2a→·b→+b→2⇒a→-b→·a→-b→=12-2×1×1×cosθ+12⇒a→-b→·a→-b→=21-cosθ⇒a→-b→2=4sin2θ2⇒a→-b→=2sin θ2  ...(2)

Therefore,

A^-B^=A^+B^tanθ2

subject: Physics
q: A person standing on an open ground hears the sound of a jet aeroplane, coming from north at an angle <math><msup><mrow><mn>60</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup></math> with ground level, but he finds the aeroplane right vertically above his position. If <math><mi>v</mi></math> is the speed of sound, speed of the plane is:
